meta.name: Environmental Protection

Sourced to Municode + County Environmental Management. First version, intentionally thin where the corpus is still being ingested; expanding.
meta.pageTitle: Title 20 — Environmental Protection · govOS · Kilo Aupuni
meta.eyebrow: 12 Stones Global · Kilo Aupuni · govOS · public service

hero.lead: A plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 20 (Environmental Protection) — the County's environmental rules (soil erosion and sediment control, pollution and nuisance protections, and related programs), how violations are fined, and who pays. Administered by the Department of Environmental Management. This module is intentionally a structural first version — much of Title 20 is expanding as the ingestion corpus is sourced verbatim; nothing here is legal advice, and we assert only what is cited.

sections.title: ★ Parcel lookup
sections.note: Environmental rules often turn on the land itself. Enter a Maui County TMK to see the parcel's designations from the Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS — including whether it's in a Special Management Area (SMA) or on Important Ag Land, both of which raise environmental review on grading and development.

source.html: Live designations: Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. Environmental programs: Dept. of Environmental Management. No parcel data is stored or fabricated — fetched live.

sections.title: 1 · What Title 20 covers
sections.note: A structural overview while the corpus is sourced verbatim. We name only what is in the Code's own structure.
sections.html: Title 20 (Environmental Protection, §§20.01.010–20.38.060) is the County's environmental-rules title, administered by the Department of Environmental Management. It carries the County's soil-erosion and sediment-control program and related environmental protections. The full chapter-by-chapter content — definitions, programs, and standards — is expanding as the ingestion session sources each chapter verbatim from Municode; until then, use the Municode link for authority and we assert only the cited items below.

source.html: Maui County Code Title 20 (Environmental Protection), §§20.01.010–20.38.060.
source.expanding: per-chapter content (definitions, programs, standards) pending corpus ingest

sections.title: 2 · Civil fines & enforcement
sections.note: How a Title 20 violation is penalized. Not legal advice.
sections.html: Title 19's enforcement chapter, §19.530.030, expressly reaches violations of MCC titles 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 19 and 20 plus related rules and permits — so an environmental violation under Title 20 can draw a notice of violation and order with civil fines, final after 30 days unless appealed to the Board of Variances and Appeals. Title 20 also supplies one of the fine measures the enforcement chapter uses: a fine of up to 1% of the project cost (§20.08.260(E)(2)) — relevant to larger environmental/grading violations.

source.html: Maui County Code Ch. 19.530 (§19.530.030 reach) + Title 20 §20.08.260(E)(2) (1%-of-project-cost fine), both sourced in the Title 19 service.

sections.title: 3 · Who pays — the charter lens
sections.note: Labeled analysis — environmental protection read through two charters. Not adopted law; an interpretive lens beside the Code.
sections.mauiCharter: Under the Maui County Charter, the Department of Environmental Management administers environmental programs and the County sets protective standards and fines by ordinance. The Charter frames environmental rules as the County's regulatory stewardship of land, water, and waste.
sections.sovereignCharter: Under the Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) lens, the ʻāina is held in trust across generations — environmental protection is a kuleana (sacred duty), not just a regulatory program. The lens reads strong protection as legitimate and necessary, while insisting that the cost of remediation fall on those who cause harm, not on the community that inherits a degraded resource.

position.label: ⚖️ Position — analysis, not law
position.text: Protect the ʻāina firmly, and make the polluter — not the public — pay for the damage and its cleanup. The 1%-of-project-cost fine is a step toward sizing penalties to the harm. Same title, read two ways.

sections.title: 4 · Rules → Code: candidates for codification
sections.intro: Provisions that, in our assessment, Title 20 should make clearer or stronger as the corpus is filled in. Citations are verbatim-checked; the recommendation is a stance beside the rule, and several items await verbatim ingest.
items: Plain-language penalty schedule. Environmental fines are spread across chapters and rules. → Codify a single readable penalty + remediation table sized to harm. expanding
Polluter-pays remediation. → Codify that the responsible party funds cleanup and monitoring, not the County's general fund.
One environmental front door. Programs split across departments confuse residents. → Document a single intake for reporting environmental harm and applying for environmental permits. expanding

source.html: Analysis grounded in MCC Title 20 + §20.08.260; most contents expanding pending verbatim ingest.

sections.text: Free now (no account): this page, the live parcel lookup, and the structural overview are all Tier 1 — free. Coming — Tier 3 (verified tools): an environmental-permit-readiness checklist and an AI Title-20 assistant will arrive as the corpus is sourced, and will be verification-gated (identity-verified). Identity is verified through the payment processor — only verified-status is stored here, never the raw ID. No live billing is enabled (Stripe keys are environment references only); any permit application happens on MAPPS.
